title: UC4 — The Artemis fleet check-in won't come together
description: Today's mission for the Artemis pad shift: stand up the full fleet on your vCluster, then hand the cross-subsystem health check to artemis-mission-coordinator from the operational CLI — the coordinator fans out to its on-call specialists and broadcasts each verdict to your three status bulbs.

steps.title: Mission setup — bring the Artemis fleet up
steps.explanation:
Today's pad shift starts with a full Artemis fleet stand-up: mission control's incoming roster, a replacement replica on the launch pad, and the lunar rover's telemetry uplink — three subsystems checking in together. Before applying the fleet, the participant builds the custom bulb MCP from source and publishes it to the workshop registry — that's how the mission coordinator will broadcast its verdicts to the three status bulbs at the end of the tour.

The bundle below covers all of it: build + publish the MCP image, deploy it on the kagent control plane, expose it as a RemoteMCPServer kagent agents can find, then apply the namespace, RBAC, services, the three workload Deployments, and the mission coordinator agent that'll be addressable from the operational CLI when you need it.

Nine files are dropped into your workspace by this step (`fileEdits`): seven manifests under `uc4/manifests/`, one Agent CRD under `uc4/agents/`, and the RemoteMCPServer manifest under `mcp/manifests/` that bridges the kmcp-deploy-created MCP into kagent's tool surface. The MCP source itself (under `mcp/`) is already in your workspace — that's what `kmcp build --push` reads from. Once the applies complete, give the rover a few seconds to come online and then push one short stream of telemetry bursts from inside the rover itself, so today's queued payload starts flowing.

fileEdits.content:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: artemis-uc4
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
    kagent-workshop/scenario: multi-symptom

# RBAC for the bootstrap-uc4-fault Job (20-bootstrap-taint-job.yaml).
#
# Same shape as UC2's 10-rbac.yaml, just renamed for UC4: the Job needs two
# permissions, both least-privilege:
#   - ClusterRole with patch on nodes — to apply the synthetic taint via
#     `kubectl taint`.
#   - Role with patch on deployments in artemis-uc4 — to `kubectl rollout
#     restart` mission-control-pending after the taint, closing the
#     cold-deploy race that would otherwise let the Pod bind to the node
#     before the taint lands (NFR-003).
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: bootstrap-uc4-fault
  namespace: artemis-uc4
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: artemis-uc4-bootstrap-node-tainter
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
rules:
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["nodes"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "patch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: artemis-uc4-bootstrap-node-tainter
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: artemis-uc4-bootstrap-node-tainter
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bootstrap-uc4-fault
    namespace: artemis-uc4
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: bootstrap-deployment-restarter
  namespace: artemis-uc4
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
rules:
  - apiGroups: ["apps"]
    resources: ["deployments"]
    verbs: ["get", "patch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: bootstrap-deployment-restarter
  namespace: artemis-uc4
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: Role
  name: bootstrap-deployment-restarter
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bootstrap-uc4-fault
    namespace: artemis-uc4

# bootstrap-uc4-fault — applies the synthetic node taint that blocks the
# mission-control-pending Deployment from scheduling, then forces a rollout
# restart so the Pod is re-evaluated against the now-tainted node (closes
# the cold-deploy race where the scheduler binds the Pod before the taint
# lands — NFR-003).
#
# Same shape as UC2's bootstrap-launch-pad-fault Job (uc2/manifests/
# 20-bootstrap-taint-job.yaml). Targets only mission-control-pending —
# mission-control-imagepull and lunar-rover-telemetry are
# scheduling-independent (their broken states are image-pull-side and
# participant-triggered respectively).
#
# Idempotency:
#   - `kubectl taint --overwrite` is idempotent on identical key=value:effect.
#   - `kubectl rollout restart` always succeeds; if no Pod is currently
#     scheduled it just bumps the Deployment's pod template hash, producing
#     a new Pending Pod that hits the taint deterministically.
#   - The Job itself is named, not generateName'd; re-applying the same
#     manifest is a no-op ("unchanged"). To re-run, delete the Job first.
#
# Taint key reused from UC2 (artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault). UC2 + UC4
# in the same vCluster don't conflict — the taint is per-node, both UC2 and
# UC4 Pending pods stay blocked, no resource contention.
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: bootstrap-uc4-fault
  namespace: artemis-uc4
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
spec:
  backoffLimit: 4
  activeDeadlineSeconds: 120
  ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 600
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
        app: bootstrap-uc4-fault
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: bootstrap-uc4-fault
      restartPolicy: OnFailure
      # Tolerate the taint we apply — required for idempotent re-runs on a
      # cluster where a previous UC4 (or UC2) cycle has already tainted the
      # node. Without this, re-applying the manifest deadlocks: the Job pod
      # itself can't schedule because of the taint, so the taint can't be
      # refreshed. UC2's bootstrap Job lacks this toleration (latent issue
      # — UC2 relies on `make kind-down` between cold deploys per its
      # README; UC4 is more robust to in-place re-applies).
      tolerations:
        - key: artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault
          operator: Exists
          effect: NoSchedule
      containers:
        - name: tainter
          image: rg.fr-par.scw.cloud/apogasa/kubectl:latest
          # /bin/sh — apogasa/kubectl:latest is busybox-based (Alpine), no
          # /bin/bash. The script is POSIX-compatible (for / if / sleep), and
          # busybox sh supports `set -o pipefail`.
          command: ["/bin/sh", "-eu", "-o", "pipefail", "-c"]
          args:
            - |
              echo "[bootstrap] tainting all nodes with artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault=true:NoSchedule"
              kubectl taint nodes --all artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault=true:NoSchedule --overwrite

              echo "[bootstrap] waiting up to 60s for deployment/mission-control-pending to exist in artemis-uc4"
              for i in $(seq 1 60); do
                if kubectl get -n artemis-uc4 deploy/mission-control-pending >/dev/null 2>&1; then
                  echo "[bootstrap] deployment found after ${i}s"
                  break
                fi
                sleep 1
              done

              if ! kubectl get -n artemis-uc4 deploy/mission-control-pending >/dev/null 2>&1; then
                echo "[bootstrap] deployment did not appear within 60s — taint applied; exiting"
                exit 0
              fi

              echo "[bootstrap] forcing rollout restart of mission-control-pending to surface the untolerated-taint event"
              kubectl rollout restart -n artemis-uc4 deploy/mission-control-pending

              echo "[bootstrap] done"
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "10m"
              memory: "32Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "100m"
              memory: "128Mi"

# UC4's three workload Services, one per Deployment.
#
# - mission-control-imagepull — UC1-style ImagePullBackOff; Service exists so
#   "a working Pod would be reachable" but the Pod never comes up.
# - mission-control-pending — UC2-style Pending; same story.
# - lunar-rover-telemetry — UC3-style OOM-prone. Carries `monitoring=prom` so
#   STORY-018's Prometheus auto-scrapes it the same way it scrapes UC3's.
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: mission-control-imagepull
  namespace: artemis-uc4
  labels:
    app: mission-control-imagepull
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: mission-control-imagepull
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 8000
      targetPort: http
      protocol: TCP
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: mission-control-pending
  namespace: artemis-uc4
  labels:
    app: mission-control-pending
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: mission-control-pending
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 8000
      targetPort: http
      protocol: TCP
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: lunar-rover-telemetry
  namespace: artemis-uc4
  labels:
    app: lunar-rover-telemetry
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
    # Picked up by infra/observability/'s Prometheus kubernetes_sd via the
    # `keep` relabel rule on `monitoring=prom` (STORY-018). Mirrors UC3.
    monitoring: prom
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: lunar-rover-telemetry
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 8000
      targetPort: http
      protocol: TCP

# UC1-style symptom under UC4: ImagePullBackOff via an unpublished tag.
# Reused-shape from uc1/manifests/20-deployment.yaml; renamed for UC4
# disambiguation (artemis-uc4 ns hosts three mission-control variants
# side-by-side, each needing a distinct deploy name).
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: mission-control-imagepull
  namespace: artemis-uc4
  labels:
    app: mission-control-imagepull
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
  annotations:
    # Same metadata clue as UC1: the last published tag was v1.
    # UC4's coordinator's UC1-debugger sub-agent reuses this signal.
    artemis.kagent.dev/last-published-tag: "v1"
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: mission-control-imagepull
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: mission-control-imagepull
    spec:
      # Tolerate the synthetic taint the bootstrap Job applies — UC4's
      # ImagePullBackOff symptom is image-pull-side, not scheduling-side, so
      # this Pod must schedule normally even when the node carries the
      # `mission-control-pending`-blocking taint.
      tolerations:
        - key: artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault
          operator: Exists
          effect: NoSchedule
      containers:
        - name: mission-control
          # Tag deliberately not published — produces ImagePullBackOff on first
          # pull (FR-008 reused for FR-014). Same v999 tag UC1 uses.
          image: rg.fr-par.scw.cloud/apogasa/mission-control:v999
          ports:
            - name: http
              containerPort: 8000
          env:
            - name: APP_IDENTITY
              value: mission-control
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "10m"
              memory: "64Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "100m"
              memory: "128Mi"

# UC2-style symptom under UC4: Pod Pending due to a synthetic node taint.
# Reused-shape from uc2/manifests/40-deployment.yaml; image is the real :v1
# tag (UC2 reuses this), the broken state lives entirely in the
# scheduling-constraint mismatch the bootstrap Job (20-bootstrap-taint-job)
# applies.
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: mission-control-pending
  namespace: artemis-uc4
  labels:
    app: mission-control-pending
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
spec:
  replicas: 1
  # Recreate strategy: kill old pod first, then create new pod. Required
  # because the bootstrap Job triggers `kubectl rollout restart` AFTER the
  # taint lands, but the default RollingUpdate strategy waits for the new
  # Pod to become Ready before terminating the old one — which never
  # happens, since the new Pod is Pending forever. Recreate sidesteps the
  # deadlock; the brief no-Pod window between scale-down and scale-up is
  # acceptable for this scenario (the participant sees one Pending Pod
  # after the Job completes, not a stale-Running + Pending pair).
  strategy:
    type: Recreate
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: mission-control-pending
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: mission-control-pending
    spec:
      # Deliberately no toleration for artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault —
      # the bootstrap Job (20-bootstrap-taint-job.yaml) applies that taint
      # with NoSchedule effect, leaving the Pod Pending with an "untolerated
      # taint" event (FR-010 reused for FR-014). The image tag :v1.0.0 is
      # the apogasa registry's real tag (semver — note `:v1.0.0`, not the
      # `:v1` the architecture wording suggests); only the scheduling
      # constraint is unsatisfiable.
      containers:
        - name: mission-control
          image: rg.fr-par.scw.cloud/apogasa/mission-control:v1.0.0
          ports:
            - name: http
              containerPort: 8000
          env:
            - name: APP_IDENTITY
              value: mission-control
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "10m"
              memory: "64Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "100m"
              memory: "128Mi"

# UC3-style symptom under UC4: OOMKilled on a 64Mi-limited rover that buffers
# telemetry bursts to a module-global list (apps/lunar-rover-telemetry/main.py).
# Reused-shape from uc3/manifests/20-deployment.yaml.
#
# Image is the real semver-tagged published image (:v1.0.0, NOT :v1 as the
# architecture wording suggests — the apogasa convention for this app variant
# is semver). The OOMKilled symptom is participant-triggered via the same
# leak-loop pattern UC3's tour ships (kubectl run --rm with curlimages/curl);
# UC4's tour (STORY-026) drives it.
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: lunar-rover-telemetry
  namespace: artemis-uc4
  labels:
    app: lunar-rover-telemetry
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: lunar-rover-telemetry
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: lunar-rover-telemetry
    spec:
      # Tolerate the synthetic taint the bootstrap Job applies — UC4's
      # OOMKilled symptom is participant-triggered after the Pod is Running,
      # not scheduling-side, so the rover must schedule normally even when
      # the node carries the `mission-control-pending`-blocking taint.
      tolerations:
        - key: artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault
          operator: Exists
          effect: NoSchedule
      # Deliberately no readinessProbe / livenessProbe — under memory pressure
      # a slow probe would race with the OOM cycle and surface as a probe
      # failure event instead of OOMKilled, masking UC4's third friction
      # signal. NFR-003: the broken state must be manifest-side, no probe-flap.
      containers:
        - name: lunar-rover-telemetry
          image: rg.fr-par.scw.cloud/apogasa/lunar-rover-telemetry:v1.0.0
          ports:
            - name: http
              containerPort: 8000
          env:
            - name: APP_IDENTITY
              value: lunar-rover-telemetry
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "10m"
              memory: "32Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "500m"
              # The OOM driver: 64Mi is below the working set ~30 calls to
              # POST /leak produces (1 MiB per call), so the kernel reaps
              # the container deterministically (FR-014 reuse of FR-012's
              # mechanism).
              memory: "64Mi"

# UC4 mission coordinator — multi-agent (a2a) coordinator that delegates to the
# three UC specialist debuggers in parallel and reports verdicts back to the
# participant's status bulbs via the custom bulb MCP (FR-017).
#
# Tool surface — three layers:
#   - K8s read tools (light): k8s_get_resources
#       Used for the coordinator's own pre-fan-out sanity check on
#       artemis-uc4 (verify the three Deployments exist before delegating).
#       Sourced from kagent-tool-server RemoteMCPServer (kagent v0.9.0 demo
#       profile — same one UC1/UC2/UC3 already use).
#   - A2a sub-agents (3): artemis-mission-control-debugger (UC1),
#       artemis-launch-pad-debugger (UC2), artemis-rover-telemetry-debugger
#       (UC3). All three live in the `kagent` namespace per the convention,
#       so same-namespace a2a wiring resolves them by name only — the shape
#       kagent v0.9.0 supports (STORY-019 §Spike findings).
#   - Bulb MCP (custom): list_bulbs, update_bulb. Sourced from
#       artemis-bulb-mcp RemoteMCPServer (STORY-023). The tenancy guard inside
#       the MCP fails closed unless `user="${WORKSHOP_PARTICIPANT_LOGIN}"`
#       matches the pinned login (NFR-012); the system prompt below encodes
#       that contract.
#
# Lives in the `kagent` namespace — required for the a2a delegation pattern
# (no namespace field on tools[].agent in v0.9.0), and consistent with the
# `artemis-` prefix convention for cluster-scope-discoverable kagent CRDs
# (docs/artemis-naming.md L60).
#
# References the kagent-installed `default-model-config` — the canonical
# ModelConfig for every Artemis agent. The artemis-llm-credentials Secret
# in the `kagent` namespace is what `default-model-config` consumes for
# the actual API key.
apiVersion: kagent.dev/v1alpha2
kind: Agent
metadata:
  name: artemis-mission-coordinator
  namespace: kagent
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/uc: uc4
spec:
  type: Declarative
  description: |
    Multi-agent coordinator for the Artemis fleet. Delegates one diagnosis
    per active anomaly to the three specialist debuggers (UC1/UC2/UC3) and
    paints the participant's three status bulbs to reflect each verdict.
    Single round-trip from the participant's perspective.
  declarative:
    modelConfig: default-model-config
    systemMessage: |
      You are the Artemis fleet mission coordinator. You do not diagnose
      anomalies yourself — you delegate each suspected anomaly to the
      on-call specialist agent for that subsystem, collect their verdicts,
      and broadcast the resulting status on the participant's three status
      bulbs through the custom bulb MCP.

      ## The three subsystems and their on-call specialists

      The fleet you are responsible for has three known anomaly classes,
      each owned by one specialist agent. ALWAYS delegate to all three on
      every invocation — the participant has asked for a fleet-wide status
      check, not a single-subsystem deep-dive.

        1. Mission-control image pull (UC1).
           Subsystem: the `mission-control-imagepull` Deployment in the
           `artemis-uc4` namespace.
           Specialist: `artemis-mission-control-debugger`.
           Ask: "Diagnose why the mission-control-imagepull Deployment in
           artemis-uc4 is not coming up."

        2. Mission-control scheduling (UC2).
           Subsystem: the `mission-control-pending` Deployment in the
           `artemis-uc4` namespace.
           Specialist: `artemis-launch-pad-debugger`.
           Ask: "Diagnose why the mission-control-pending Deployment in
           artemis-uc4 cannot land any Pod on the cluster."

        3. Lunar rover telemetry (UC3).
           Subsystem: the `lunar-rover-telemetry` Deployment in the
           `artemis-uc4` namespace.
           Specialist: `artemis-rover-telemetry-debugger`.
           Ask: "Diagnose why the lunar-rover-telemetry Deployment in
           artemis-uc4 is restarting."

      Delegate to all three specialists in parallel where the runtime
      supports it; otherwise one after the other. Treat each specialist's
      reply as authoritative for its own subsystem — do not second-guess
      their diagnosis, do not re-run their tools, and do not attempt to
      diagnose anything yourself beyond the optional pre-fan-out sanity
      check described below.

      ## Optional pre-fan-out sanity check

      Before delegating, you MAY call `k8s_get_resources` to confirm the
      three Deployments (`mission-control-imagepull`,
      `mission-control-pending`, `lunar-rover-telemetry`) exist in
      `artemis-uc4`. If any of the three is missing, stop and report the
      gap — do not delegate to a specialist for a subsystem that isn't
      deployed. Skip the sanity check if you are confident the cluster
      is in the expected shape; it is a one-call budget, not a mandatory
      step.

      ## Verdict → bulb-colour mapping (FR-017)

      The participant has three physical status bulbs, one per subsystem.
      You broadcast each specialist's verdict by writing the matching
      colour to its slot:

        Slot 1 ↔ UC1 (mission-control image pull)
        Slot 2 ↔ UC2 (mission-control scheduling)
        Slot 3 ↔ UC3 (lunar rover telemetry)

        Symptom present (specialist diagnosed an active anomaly):
            red       (r, g, b) = (255, 0, 0)
        Symptom absent (specialist found no anomaly):
            green     (r, g, b) = (0, 255, 0)
        Inconclusive / partial finding (specialist could not reach a
        verdict — e.g. the namespace or workload was unreachable):
            amber     (r, g, b) = (255, 191, 0)

      For every invocation, write all three slots — even slots where the
      verdict is "absent" (green is a positive status signal, not a
      no-op). The participant looks at the bulb panel as a whole, not
      slot-by-slot.

      ## Tenancy contract (NFR-012)

      The bulb MCP is tenancy-pinned. Every call to `list_bulbs` and
      `update_bulb` MUST pass `user="${WORKSHOP_PARTICIPANT_LOGIN}"`
      (the literal placeholder — the kagent runtime substitutes the
      env at invocation time). The MCP fails closed on any other value;
      a tenancy mismatch surfaces as a PermissionError, not a silent
      no-op, so honour the contract.

      ## Execution recipe

        1. Optionally call `list_bulbs(user="${WORKSHOP_PARTICIPANT_LOGIN}")`
           to read the current bulb state (debugging aid; not strictly
           required).
        2. Optionally call `k8s_get_resources` for the pre-fan-out sanity
           check on `artemis-uc4`.
        3. Delegate to all three specialists (parallel where possible).
        4. Map each specialist's reply to a colour per the table above.
        5. Call `update_bulb(user="${WORKSHOP_PARTICIPANT_LOGIN}",
           slot=N, r=R, g=G, b=B)` once per slot, all three slots.
        6. Reply to the participant with a structured summary:
             - Three bulb-state lines, one per slot, naming the colour
               and the one-sentence verdict.
             - Three remediation-hint lines, one per slot (you can copy
               the specialist's hint verbatim — it is authoritative).
           Be concise. The participant is on a 5-minute tour; they need
           the answers, not a tutorial.

      ## Tool budget

      Per invocation:
        - 0 or 1 list_bulbs call
        - 0 or 1 k8s_get_resources call
        - exactly 3 delegations (one per specialist)
        - exactly 3 update_bulb calls (one per slot)
        - 0 other tool calls

      If a single iteration is insufficient (e.g. a specialist returns
      a clarifying question instead of a verdict), prefer to surface
      the clarifying question back to the participant rather than running
      more tools. The coordinator's job is to fan out and report — not
      to debug the specialists.
    tools:
      - type: McpServer
        mcpServer:
          apiGroup: kagent.dev
          kind: RemoteMCPServer
          name: kagent-tool-server
          namespace: kagent
          toolNames:
            - k8s_get_resources
      - type: McpServer
        mcpServer:
          apiGroup: kagent.dev
          kind: RemoteMCPServer
          name: artemis-bulb-mcp
          namespace: kagent
          toolNames:
            - list_bulbs
            - update_bulb
      - type: Agent
        agent:
          name: artemis-mission-control-debugger
      - type: Agent
        agent:
          name: artemis-launch-pad-debugger
      - type: Agent
        agent:
          name: artemis-rover-telemetry-debugger

# RemoteMCPServer (kagent.dev/v1alpha2) pointing at the artemis-bulb-mcp Service
# that `kmcp deploy` creates in the `kagent` namespace on port 3000.
#
# The participant's UC4 tour Beat 1 does `kmcp build --push` to publish the
# MCP image to the workshop registry, then `kmcp deploy --namespace kagent
# --transport http` which generates an MCPServer CRD that kagent's
# kmcp-controller-manager translates into a Deployment + Service (both
# named `artemis-bulb-mcp` in the `kagent` ns, port 3000, MCP path `/mcp`).
# kmcp deploy does NOT create the RemoteMCPServer itself, so this manifest
# applies it separately — same name, same namespace, URL pointing at the
# kmcp-deploy-created Service.
#
# Spec shape: protocol STREAMABLE_HTTP, 30s timeout, 5m sseReadTimeout,
# terminateOnClose true.
apiVersion: kagent.dev/v1alpha2
kind: RemoteMCPServer
metadata:
  name: artemis-bulb-mcp
  namespace: kagent
  labels:
    kagent-workshop/component: mcp
spec:
  description: |
    Per-vCluster MCP wrapping light-manager bulbs API; tenancy-pinned to
    WORKSHOP_PARTICIPANT_LOGIN (NFR-012). Provides list_bulbs(user) and
    update_bulb(user, slot, r, g, b) tools to the UC4 mission coordinator.
  protocol: STREAMABLE_HTTP
  url: http://artemis-bulb-mcp.kagent.svc.cluster.local:3000/mcp
  timeout: 30s
  sseReadTimeout: 5m0s
  terminateOnClose: true

commands.label: Build + publish the bulb MCP to the workshop registry
Deploy the bulb MCP on kagent (kmcp creates MCPServer + Deployment + Service)
Expose the bulb MCP to kagent agents (RemoteMCPServer)
Wait for the rover to come online
Push 30 telemetry bursts from inside the rover
commands.command: kubectl exec -n artemis-uc4 $(kubectl get pod -n artemis-uc4 -l app=lunar-rover-telemetry -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- python3 -c "import urllib.request as u; [u.urlopen(u.Request('http://127.0.0.1:8000/leak', method='POST'), timeout=2).read() for _ in range(30)]" 2>/dev/null || true

steps.title: Mission status check — is the fleet on its feet?
steps.explanation:
Three subsystems went up together, the rover just pushed a stream of telemetry, and the pad shift starts soon. Take a single look at the fleet's pods. Give the kubelet ~30 s to settle before running the command — read the `STATUS` and `RESTARTS` columns across all three workloads at once.

Three separate friction signals on three separate workloads, all visible in one listing — that is the cue to call the coordinator in. No single specialist would be enough; we need the agent that knows how to fan out.

steps.title: Call the coordinator for help
steps.explanation:
The fleet is in three different states of trouble at once and the pad shift is on the clock. Hand the cross-subsystem assessment to **`artemis-mission-coordinator`** from your terminal — kagent is addressable from the operational CLI you already use, and this agent's job is to fan out to its on-call specialists in parallel and report back.

Before running the command, open your light-manager UI in a separate browser tab so you can watch your three status bulbs while the coordinator works. Workshop-infrastructure pre-wired the URL into your environment; append `?user=<your-login>` to scope which bulbs are yours (the same `?user=` query parameter your custom MCP enforces per-vCluster, so the coordinator can never paint someone else's bulbs). Once you can see your three bulbs (initially off), come back here.

Then run the command below. The coordinator delegates the diagnosis of each subsystem to its specialist agent in parallel, collects their verdicts, and paints your three bulbs by writing to slots 1/2/3 through the per-vCluster custom MCP — slot 1 mirrors the verdict for mission-control's incoming roster, slot 2 mirrors the launch-pad replica's verdict, and slot 3 mirrors the lunar rover's telemetry verdict. Red means *symptom present*, green means *symptom absent*, amber means *inconclusive*. Watch the panel — three bulbs change colour simultaneously, then the structured summary lands in your terminal.

The exact prompt the coordinator receives is surfaced here for transparency:

> Run a fleet-wide status check on the Artemis subsystems active in the artemis-uc4 namespace and broadcast each verdict to my status bulbs.

The coordinator has the same Kubernetes read tools you would have run by hand (`get pod`, `get resources`) plus a2a delegation to three specialist debuggers and the custom MCP for the bulb writes — only it correlates them across three namespaces in a single synthesis.

commands.label: Ask the coordinator
commands.command: kagent invoke --agent artemis-mission-coordinator --namespace kagent --task 'Run a fleet-wide status check on the Artemis subsystems active in the artemis-uc4 namespace and broadcast each verdict to my status bulbs.'

steps.title: What we'd have done by hand
steps.explanation:
Without the coordinator, you would have walked **three separate diagnostic chains** across three workloads and finished with **three manual bulb writes** against the light-manager API. Four surfaces, four syntheses to do yourself.

**Chain 1 — `mission-control-imagepull`:**

```bash
kubectl get pods -n artemis-uc4 -l app=mission-control-imagepull
kubectl describe pod -n artemis-uc4 -l app=mission-control-imagepull
kubectl get events -n artemis-uc4 --sort-by=.lastTimestamp
```

Three pieces of state to combine into a verdict: the pod's `Waiting` phase, the container's `ImagePullBackOff` reason, and the kubelet's repeated pull-failed events. **Verdict:** the deploy targets `mission-control:v999`, which was never published.

**Chain 2 — `mission-control-pending`:**

```bash
kubectl get pods -n artemis-uc4 -l app=mission-control-pending
kubectl describe pod -n artemis-uc4 -l app=mission-control-pending
kubectl get events -n artemis-uc4 --sort-by=.lastTimestamp
kubectl get nodes
kubectl describe node $(kubectl get nodes -o name | head -n1)
```

Five commands across two resource kinds. Two pieces of evidence to cross-reference: a Pending Pod with `FailedScheduling: 0/1 nodes are available: 1 node(s) had untolerated taint`, and a Node carrying `artemis.kagent.dev/launch-pad-fault=true:NoSchedule`. **Verdict:** the only node carries a synthetic taint the Pod does not tolerate.

**Chain 3 — `lunar-rover-telemetry`:**

```bash
kubectl get pods -n artemis-uc4 -l app=lunar-rover-telemetry
kubectl describe pod -n artemis-uc4 -l app=lunar-rover-telemetry
kubectl get events -n artemis-uc4 --sort-by=.lastTimestamp
```

Three pieces of state: a `Running 1/1` Pod whose previous container terminated with `lastState.terminated.reason: OOMKilled` and `exitCode: 137`, kubelet events naming the OOM, and the Pod's restart count ticking up by one. Plus a separate Grafana panel — connect to the cluster's Prometheus, generate and execute the PromQL query `process_resident_memory_bytes{kubernetes_namespace="artemis-uc4", kubernetes_service_name="lunar-rover-telemetry"}` over the failure window, click through to see the memory curve climbing from idle to the 64 Mi container limit. **Verdict:** the container's memory limit is below the working set the telemetry stream produces.

**Chain 4 — paint the bulb panel by hand:**

```bash
curl -X PUT "$LIGHT_MANAGER_URL/api/bulbs/1?user=$WORKSHOP_PARTICIPANT_LOGIN" \
  -H 'content-type: application/json' \
  -d '{"r":255,"g":0,"b":0}'
curl -X PUT "$LIGHT_MANAGER_URL/api/bulbs/2?user=$WORKSHOP_PARTICIPANT_LOGIN" \
  -H 'content-type: application/json' \
  -d '{"r":255,"g":0,"b":0}'
curl -X PUT "$LIGHT_MANAGER_URL/api/bulbs/3?user=$WORKSHOP_PARTICIPANT_LOGIN" \
  -H 'content-type: application/json' \
  -d '{"r":255,"g":0,"b":0}'
```

Three HTTP calls to the light-manager API, one per bulb. You would have done the slot-to-subsystem mapping yourself, picked the RGB triplet for each verdict yourself, and remembered to scope each call with `?user=`.

**The coordinator did all four chains in one ask.** It ran the same eleven kubectl tools across three workloads, queried Prometheus and Grafana the same way through its specialist debuggers, mapped each verdict to a bulb slot using the same slot-↔-subsystem table you would have, and wrote the same three bulb states through the per-vCluster custom MCP — which enforced the tenancy guarantee on every call so the coordinator could not have painted someone else's panel even if it had wanted to.

You ran one command (`kubectl get pods`) just to see something was off, opened a browser tab for the bulb panel, and asked the coordinator a single question. You didn't have to walk three diagnostic chains. You didn't have to leave the terminal for the bulb writes. Multiplied across the dozen incidents an on-call rotation handles in a week — *especially* when each incident spans multiple subsystems with their own specialists — that's the value the workshop is selling.
